Thus, it would seem that choosing between these scenarios is largely a matter of biological interpretation. However, there are some qualitative differences between the two scenarios. Since the synaptic inputs are all of the same order as the threshold, it is harder to obtain states with low mean rates in both the excitatory and inhibitory populations. To achieve low rates, the ratio between the external inputs to the two populations (I/E in the notation of equations 2.4 and 2.5) has to be close to the ratio of their thresholds $\theta_{\mathrm{I}} / \theta_{\mathrm{E}}$. More important, the weak-synapses scenario of Figure 17 breaks down in the presence of inhomogeneity in the local thresholds. In this case, the population rates are incapable of accommodating the different thresholds. As a result, in the case of inhomogeneous thresholds, when K increases, the network state becomes increasingly frozen; neurons with high thresholds become inactive, whereas neurons with low ones fire close to saturation. This freezing occurs as soon as the width of the inhomogeneity in threshold is larger than $1 / \sqrt{\mathrm{K}}$. In contrast, in the scenario of
strong synapses, the state becomes frozen only when the inhomogeneity is large compared to 1. Note that in the case of Figure 17, the external input is of the same order as the net input to the cell. Equally important is the fact that in the weak scenario case and homogeneous networks, the collective time constants are of the same order as the single cell time constant so that the network will not exhibit the phenomenon of fast tracking predicted in our theory.

Finally, the model can be generalized to a model with synaptic strengths that scale as $\mathrm{K}^{-\alpha}$, with $0<\alpha<1$. Of course, these models can be distinguished from the present model only in the large K limit. In this limit, the net average inputs into the populations scale as $\mathrm{K}^{1-\alpha}$, while the quenched and temporal fluctuations in the inputs scale as $\mathrm{K}^{1 / 2-\alpha}$. Therefore the leading order in the inputs has to cancel, leading to the balance condition. For any $\alpha$, this leads to asynchronous chaotic activity in a homogeneous network, similar to the case $\alpha=1 / 2$. However, if we introduce a distribution of the threshold with width of order 1 , we have to distinguish two regimes, apart from $\alpha=1 / 2$ of our model. If $\alpha>1 / 2$, the fluctuations in the input decrease with K so that the network goes to the frozen state in the large K limit. On the other hand, if $\alpha<1 / 2$, the fluctuations grow with K , and therefore the inhomogeneity in the threshold becomes negligible in the large K limit. Thus, a network with inhomogeneous thresholds will act in the same way as a network with homogeneous thresholds. Specifically for low rates, the rate distribution will become narrow. Thus, only for a network with synaptic strengths of order $1 / \sqrt{\mathrm{K}}$ is there a nontrivial interaction between the fluctuation in the input and the threshold inhomogeneities.
10.3 Comparison with Other Netw ork Models. Some of our results are consistent with those of the integrate-and-fire network models of Tsodyks and Sejnowski (1995) and Amit and Brunel (1997a, 1997b). Although constructing an exact mean-field theory for the integrate-and-fire dynamics similar to the one presented here for binary units is much more difficult, we believe that most of the predictions of our mean-field theory are applicable to the integrate-and-fire dynamics as well, provided that the same connectivity architecture and scaling of parameters with N and K are used. However, a direct comparison between our theory and the results of Tsodyks and Sejnowski (1995) and Amit and Brunel (1997a, 1997b) is difficult because of their introduction of stochasticity in the network, the combination of mechanisms such as resetting potential close to threshold, and the lack of full, explicit specification of scaling of parameters with N and K . Tsodyks and Sejnowski (1995) show numerically that their model is capable of "fast switching" in response to a fast change in the external stimulus. This may be related to the fast tracking predicted in our model. The fact that our model does not respond quickly to a sudden switching of the stimulus (see Figure 13) is probably a result of the dynamics of binary neurons. However,
the switching time constants observed in Tsodyks and Sejnowski (1995) is of the same order as the single-cell integration time constant, while the fast tracking should occur on a much shorter time constant. In recent numerical simulations of integrate-and-fire networks, Amit and Brunel (1997b) showed that the strength of the average cross-correlations decreases as N increases (keeping the connectivity index constant). However, they do not show whether as N increases, the variability in the single cell remains the same. If this would be the case, their results are consistent with our predictions regarding asynchronous chaotic state.
10.4 Biological Implications. With regard to the biological systems, we should reemphasize that most likely temporal irregularity is a result of several mechanisms, including those mentioned in section 1. Our discussion makes it clear that even with regard to deterministic network mechanism in a finite system, the temporally irregular state is likely to be at best intermediate between the synchronized and the balanced chaotic states. An important question is whether external input is large relative to net input to a cortical cell. Recent experimental findings of Ferster, Chung, and Wheat (1996) in cat primary visual cortex suggest that the input from the lateral geniculate nucleus (LGN) to layer 4 cortical cells are in fact a fraction of the net input. Stratford, Tarczy-Hornoch, Martin, Bannister, and Jack (1996) show that the total strength of the LGN synapses is about 2.5 to 3 times smaller than the total strength of the excitatory feedback synapses from layer 4 cells; however, this study does not measure the strength of the feedback from the inhibitory interneurons, so it does not allow for the estimation of the net feedback. Further experimental clarification of this issue is called for. Measurements of the distribution of time-averaged rates within a local population of neurons and the change in its shape when the overall level of response increases, similar to those of Figure 11B, would be an interesting test of the underlying statistical characteristics of the network spatiotemporal fluctuations.
10.5 Future Work. On theoretical grounds, our work raises several interesting issues worth pursuing. First, it would be important to know whether the theory of the balanced state applies also to networks with more interesting connectivity architecture. Thus, it would be interesting to extend our theory to networks that model associative memory or hypercolumns in visual cortex. It is important to study the consequences of nonlinearities of synaptic summations, for example, by treating synaptic inputs as conductance changes instead of currents.

In considering the functional implications of our theory, it is important to distinguish between the sensitivity of a chaotic autonomous system to changes in its initial condition and its ability to lock to a changing external drive. The analysis of tracking capabilities of our network shows that the macroscopic state of the network responds fast to changing input. In the
case of a homogeneous input, it can be shown that the microscopic state is not tightly locked to the changing stimulus. On the other hand, preliminary analysis (van Vreeswijk \& Sompolinsky, 1998) shows that in the case of spatially inhomogeneous input fluctuations, the microscopic state of the network will tightly lock to the stimulus temporal variations. These findings are consistent with recent findings that cortical cells respond highly reliably to the fluctuations in the stimulus (Bair \& Koch, 1996; Britten, Shadlen, Newsom, \& Movshon, 1992). Elucidation of the computational aspects of balanced states in neuronal networks is a challenging issue.

Recently Markram and Tsodyks (1996) have shown that the synapses between cortical pyramidal cells show a marked degree of depression. It should be investigated how such dynamical synapses affect the balanced state. If one assumes synaptic depression between the excitatory-to-excitatory synapses only, and facilitation between the synapses from the inhibitory to the excitatory and from the excitatory to inhibitory populations (Thomson, West, \& Deuchars, 1995; Thomson, West, Hahn, \& Deuchars, 1996), the equilibrium rates in the network decrease, relative to those in a network without facilitation. This synaptic depression and facilitation also has the effect that the constraints on the synaptic strengths (see equations 4.9 and 4.10) can be relaxed. Because the synaptic depression and facilitation become effective only on a time scale that is as slow as or slower than the membrane time constant, the response of such a network to an external input that changes with time is more complicated than in the model studied here. If the input is suddenly increased by a small amount, the network rates increase to the rate the network would have in equilibrium if the synaptic strengths were not changed in a time of order $1 / \sqrt{\mathrm{K}}$ and then, on a much slower time scale, the rates decrease due to the change in the synaptic strengths.

Since in the balanced state the finite K corrections of the rates are determined by both the first and the second moment of input, the change in rate due to synaptic depression or facilitation depends not only on the average change in the synaptic strength but also on its fluctuation. Thus, synaptic depression due to a change in the height of the excitatory postsynaptive potentials (EPSPs), but without a change in the probability of release, will affect the rates differently from synaptic depression that leaves the height of the EPSPs unaffected but decreases the probability of release, even if both mechanisms result in the same average depression. Another effect of synaptic depression to take into account is that the effect of a spike is decreased if it follows shortly after the preceding spike. This will decrease the fluctuations in the input, relative to the fluctuations in the activity of the presynaptic cells. Facilitation will have the opposite effect, since it increases the effect of a spike if it closely follows the previous one. These issues warrant further study.

## A ppendix A: Derivation of the Mean-Field Theory

A. 1 Population Rates. We first consider the population-averaged activities $\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{E}}(\mathrm{t})$ and $\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{I}}(\mathrm{t})$ in the limit of large $\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{E}}$ and $\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{I}}$ and finite K . We first assume that each cell in the kth population is updated stochastically at a rate $\tau_{\mathrm{k}}$. When a cell is updated, it moves to the active state if its total input is above threshold. Otherwise its updated state is 0 . It is convenient to define a time-dependent local rate variable,

$$
\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{k}}^{\mathrm{i}}(\mathrm{t})=\left\langle\sigma_{\mathrm{k}}^{\mathrm{i}}(\mathrm{t})\right\rangle .
$$

Here, the symbol $\langle\ldots\rangle$ does not mean average over time, as in equation 5.4 and thereafter. Instead, it means average over all initial conditions that are consistent with given values for $\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{k}}(0)$ and also over the random sequence of update times. It is well known that the rate of a binary variable that obeys the update rule satisfies the following continuous time dynamics (Ginzburg \& Sompolinsky, 1994; Glauber, 1963),

$$
\tau_{\mathrm{k}} \frac{\mathrm{~d}}{\mathrm{dt}} \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{k}}^{\mathrm{i}}(\mathrm{t})=-\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{k}}^{\mathrm{i}}(\mathrm{t})+\Theta\left(\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{k}}^{\mathrm{i}}(\mathrm{t})\right),
$$

where $\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{k}}^{\mathrm{i}}(\mathrm{t})$ is the total synaptic input into a cell i in the kth population relative to its threshold and is given in our case by equation 2.2. If a cell receives $\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{E}}(\mathrm{t})$ and $\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{I}}(\mathrm{t})$ excitatory and inhibitory feedback inputs, respectively, then its input is

$$
\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{k}}^{\mathrm{i}}(\mathrm{t})=\sqrt{\mathrm{K}} \mathrm{~J}_{\mathrm{k} 0} \mathrm{~m}_{0}+\frac{\mathrm{J}_{\mathrm{kE}}}{\sqrt{\mathrm{~K}}} \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{E}}(\mathrm{t})+\frac{\mathrm{J}_{\mathrm{kI}}}{\sqrt{\mathrm{~K}}} \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{I}}(\mathrm{t})-\theta_{\mathrm{k}} .
$$

The main assumption underlying the mean-field theory is that the activities of the different input cells to a given cell are uncorrelated. Technically, this holds rigorously provided that $\mathrm{K} \ll \log \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{k}}$ (Derrida et al., 1987). Using this assumption, the population average of equation A. 2 yields the following mean-field equations for the population activities,

$$
\tau_{\mathrm{k}} \frac{\mathrm{~d}}{\mathrm{dt}} \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{k}}(\mathrm{t})=-\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{k}}(\mathrm{t})+\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{k}}\left(\mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{E}}(\mathrm{t}), \mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{I}}(\mathrm{t})\right),
$$
where $\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{k}}$ denotes the probability that the updating cell at time t will be in an updated active state. It is given by

$$
\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{k}}\left(\mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{E}}, \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{I}}\right)=\sum_{\mathrm{n}_{1}, \mathrm{n}_{2}=0}^{\infty} \mathrm{p}_{1}\left(\mathrm{n}_{1}\right) \mathrm{p}_{2}\left(\mathrm{n}_{2}\right) \Theta\left(\sqrt{\mathrm{K}} \mathrm{~J}_{\mathrm{k} 0} \mathrm{~m}_{0}+\sum_{\mathrm{l}} \frac{\mathrm{~J}_{\mathrm{kl}}}{\sqrt{\mathrm{~K}}} \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{l}}-\theta_{\mathrm{k}}\right),
$$

where $p_{l}(n)$ is the probability that a cell receives $n$ active inputs from the lth population.

For $\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{E}}, \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{I}} \rightarrow \infty$ the probability of s synapses of population l projecting to a cell is $\mathrm{K}^{\mathrm{s}} \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{K}} / \mathrm{s}$ !. On average each of these synapses has a probability $\mathrm{m}_{1}$ to be active, hence,

Equations A. 4 through A. 6 define the mean-field equations for the population activity levels for finite $K$. The average values of $n_{E}$ and $n_{I}$ satisfy $\left\langle\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{k}}\right\rangle=\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{k}} \mathrm{K}$. The standard deviations $\sigma\left(\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{E}}\right)$ and $\sigma\left(\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{I}}\right)$ are given by $\sigma\left(\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{k}}\right)=\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{k}} \mathrm{K}$. ## Appendix B: Determinstic Update Rules

The general form of equation A. 4 is usually derived for a binary variable that is updated stochastically at a rate $\tau_{\mathrm{k}}$. One might therefore argue that the irregular firing in our model is due to the stochasticity of the update times of the model neurons. To show that this is not the case, we define here a completely deterministic dynamic model and show that it leads to exactly the same equations for the mean rates of activity as those given above.

Consider the same network model, except that a neuron i of population k is updated at times $\mathrm{t}=\left(\mathrm{n}+\delta_{\mathrm{k}}^{\mathrm{i}}\right) \tau_{\mathrm{k}}$ with $\mathrm{n}=0,1,2, \ldots$ and $\delta_{\mathrm{k}}^{\mathrm{i}}$ is randomly chosen between 0 and 1 . Let $\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{k}}^{+}(\mathrm{t})$ be the probability that the neuron of population k, updated at timet, goes into (or stays in) the active state. Since all neurons of population k are updated exactly once between times $\mathrm{t}-\tau_{\mathrm{k}}$ and $\mathrm{t}, \mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{k}}(\mathrm{t})$ is given by

$$
\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{k}}(\mathrm{t})=\frac{1}{\tau_{\mathrm{k}}} \int_{0}^{\tau_{\mathrm{k}}} \mathrm{dt}^{\prime} \mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{k}}^{+}\left(\mathrm{t}-\mathrm{t}^{\prime}\right)
$$
Going through arguments similar to those shown above, one can show that $\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{k}}$ satisfies

$$
\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{k}}(\mathrm{t})=\frac{1}{\tau_{\mathrm{k}}} \int_{0}^{\tau_{\mathrm{k}}} \mathrm{dt}^{\prime} \mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{k}}\left(\mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{E}}\left(\mathrm{t}-\mathrm{t}^{\prime}\right), \mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{I}}\left(\mathrm{t}-\mathrm{t}^{\prime}\right)\right)
$$

with $\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{k}}$ given by equation A.5.
If we introduce inhomogeneities in the rate with which the cells are updated so that cell i of population is updated at times $\mathrm{t}=\left(\mathrm{n}+\delta_{\mathrm{k}}^{\mathrm{i}}\right) \tau_{\mathrm{i}}^{\mathrm{k}}$, where $\tau_{\mathrm{i}}^{\mathrm{k}}$ has a probability $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{k}}(\tau) \mathrm{d} \tau$ of being between $\tau$ and $\tau+\mathrm{d} \tau$, we find that $\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{k}}$ evolves as

$$
\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{k}}(\mathrm{t})=\int_{0}^{\infty} \mathrm{d} \tau \frac{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{k}}(\tau)}{\tau} \int_{0}^{\tau} \mathrm{dt}^{\prime} \mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{k}}\left(\mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{E}}\left(\mathrm{t}-\mathrm{t}^{\prime}\right), \mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{I}}\left(\mathrm{t}-\mathrm{t}^{\prime}\right)\right)
$$
For $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{k}}(\mathrm{t})=\mathrm{te}^{-\mathrm{t} / \tau_{\mathrm{k}}} / \tau_{\mathrm{k}}^{2}$, this can be written as

$$
\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{k}}(\mathrm{t})=\frac{1}{\tau_{\mathrm{k}}} \int_{0}^{\infty} \mathrm{dt}^{\prime} \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{t} / \tau_{\mathrm{k}}} \mathrm{~F}_{\mathrm{k}}\left(\mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{E}}\left(\mathrm{t}-\mathrm{t}^{\prime}\right), \mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{I}}\left(\mathrm{t}-\mathrm{t}^{\prime}\right)\right)
$$

and this is equivalent to equation A.4.
Thus, in this completely deterministic model, the mean rates $\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{k}}$ satisfy exactly the same equations as the model with stochastic updating. This also holds true for the other mean-field equations of the model.

comments.title: Clarity of the model's scaling argument in the Introduction
comments.paragraph: An essential ingredient of our model is the introduction of strong connections among the units. A cell is connected, on the average, to K other cells, and K is large. However, the gap between the threshold of the cell and its resting potential is only of the order of $\sqrt{\mathrm{K}}$ excitatory inputs. Thus, the network will saturate unless a dynamically developed balance between the excitatory inputs and the inhibitory inputs to a cell emerges.
comments.quote: An essential ingredient of our model is the introduction of strong connections among the units. A cell is connected, on the average, to K other cells, and K is large. However, the gap between the threshold of the cell and its resting potential is only of the order of $\sqrt{\mathrm{K}}$ excitatory inputs. Thus, the network will saturate unless a dynamically developed balance between the excitatory inputs and the inhibitory inputs to a cell emerges.
comments.message: I initially had trouble with the scaling argument in this introductory paragraph because the statement "the gap between the threshold of the cell and its resting potential is only of the order of $\sqrt{K}$ excitatory inputs" appears before the synaptic-strength scaling is specified. However, reading on to section 2 makes clear that the formal model assumes thresholds of order one and synaptic strengths of order $1/\sqrt{K}$, so that the rest–threshold gap in units of a single EPSP is indeed $O(\sqrt{K})$, and the saturation/balance argument is consistent. That said, this is a central conceptual point, and the introduction is the first place the reader encounters it. It might improve the ease of first-pass comprehension if the authors briefly foreshadow here that, in the detailed model, individual synaptic strengths scale as $1/\sqrt{K}$ while thresholds remain $O(1)$, and that this is what underlies the statement that the threshold corresponds to $O(\sqrt{K})$ excitatory inputs and therefore a vanishing fraction of the $K$ available inputs.

comments.title: Clarification on the mean-field theory's regime of validity
comments.paragraph: The mean-field theory is exact in the limit of large network size, N, and $1 \ll \mathrm{~K} \ll \mathrm{~N}$. In section 4 the behavior of the population rates in the balanced state is studied. Section 5is devoted to the spatial and temporal distribution of activity within the network.
comments.quote: The mean-field theory is exact in the limit of large network size, N, and $1 \ll \mathrm{~K} \ll \mathrm{~N}$. In section 4 the behavior of the population rates in the balanced state is studied. Section 5is devoted to the spatial and temporal distribution of activity within the network.
comments.message: In several places the paper uses different formulations for the asymptotic regime underlying the mean-field theory: the Introduction states that the theory is "exact in the limit of large network size, N, and $1 \ll K \ll N$," Section 3 emphasizes a limit with $K$ fixed as $N \to \infty$ followed by a large-$K$ approximation, and Appendix A.1 notes that the uncorrelated-input assumption "holds rigorously provided that $K \ll \log N_k$" (citing Derrida et al.). At first reading it is not entirely clear how these conditions are meant to fit together, nor in which precise sense the mean-field description is "exact" as opposed to being a controlled approximation. Clarifying the intended ordering of limits (first $N \to \infty$ at fixed $K$, then large $K$), the role of the $K \ll \log N_k$ condition as a sufficient rigorous criterion rather than a general scaling assumption, and what is assumed when one informally writes $1 \ll K \ll N$ would help readers understand more precisely the regime of validity of the subsequent large‑$K$ results.

comments.title: Inconsistent definition of connection probability in Sec. 2
comments.paragraph: The connection between the ith postsynaptic neuron of the kth population and the jth presynaptic neuron of the lth population, denoted $\mathrm{J}_{\mathrm{kl}}^{\mathrm{ij}}$, is $\mathrm{J}_{\mathrm{kl}} / \sqrt{\mathrm{K}}$ with probability $\mathrm{K} / \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{k}}$ and zero otherwise. Here $\mathrm{k}, \mathrm{l}=1,2$. The synaptic constants $\mathrm{J}_{\mathrm{k} 1}$ are positive and $\mathrm{J}_{\mathrm{k} 2}$ negative. Thus, on average, K excitatory and K inhibitory neurons project to each neuron.
comments.quote: The connection between the ith postsynaptic neuron of the kth population and the jth presynaptic neuron of the lth population, denoted $\mathrm{J}_{\mathrm{kl}}^{\mathrm{ij}}$, is $\mathrm{J}_{\mathrm{kl}} / \sqrt{\mathrm{K}}$ with probability $\mathrm{K} / \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{k}}$ and zero otherwise. Here $\mathrm{k}, \mathrm{l}=1,2$. The synaptic constants $\mathrm{J}_{\mathrm{k} 1}$ are positive and $\mathrm{J}_{\mathrm{k} 2}$ negative. Thus, on average, K excitatory and K inhibitory neurons project to each neuron.
comments.message: The definition of the connection probability in Section 2 seems to contain a subscript error that conflicts with the stated average in-degree and with later mean-field derivations. As written, the text specifies that $J_{kl}^{ij}$ is nonzero "with probability $K/N_k$," i.e. depending on the postsynaptic population size, and then states "Thus, on average, K excitatory and K inhibitory neurons project to each neuron." For general $N_1$ and $N_2$, a probability $K/N_k$ would give an expected number of inputs from population $l$ equal to $N_l K/N_k$, which equals $K$ only if $N_l=N_k$. Moreover, Appendix A and Section 3 later assume that the number of synapses from population $l$ onto a given neuron is Poisson with mean $K$ (eq. A.6), and use this to derive $u_k=\sqrt{K}\sum_l J_{kl} m_l + \dots$ and $\alpha_k=\sum_l J_{kl}^2 m_l$. Those forms are consistent with a connectivity rule in which each presynaptic neuron in population $l$ connects with probability $K/N_l$, yielding a mean of $K$ inputs from each population, and are not consistent with $K/N_k$. It would therefore be helpful to correct the connectivity definition to make explicit that the connection probability is $K/N_l$ (depending on the presynaptic population size), which matches both the verbal statement about "on average, K excitatory and K inhibitory neurons project to each neuron" and the subsequent mean-field analysis.

comments.title: Sign convention for quenched noise in Sec 5
comments.quote: q_{\mathrm{k}}=\int \mathrm{Dx}\left[\mathrm{H}\left(\frac{-\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{k}}+\sqrt{\beta_{\mathrm{k}}} \mathrm{x}}{\sqrt{\alpha_{\mathrm{k}}-\beta_{\mathrm{k}}}}\right)\right]^{2} ... \times \exp \left(-\mathrm{t} / \tau_{\mathrm{k}}\right) \int \mathrm{Dx}\left[\mathrm{H}\left(\frac{-\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{k}}-\sqrt{\beta_{\mathrm{k}}(\mathrm{t}+\tau)} \mathrm{x}}{\sqrt{\alpha_{\mathrm{k}}-\beta_{\mathrm{k}}(\mathrm{t}+\tau)}}\right)\right]^{2}.
comments.message: I initially had trouble with the opposite signs of the $\sqrt{\beta_k}\,x$ term in equations 5.12 and 5.17, which at first seemed to imply different conventions for the quenched Gaussian variable $x$. However, since $x$ is a standard normal with symmetric measure $Dx$, the sign of this term can always be absorbed by redefining $x \to -x$, and the resulting integrals (and hence the statistics of $m_k^i$ and $q_k(\tau)$) are unchanged. If you think readers might dwell on this point, it could be helpful to mention explicitly that the sign of the quenched Gaussian factor is arbitrary.

comments.paragraph: The time average of the total excitatory (inhibitory) component is itself sampled from a gaussian distribution with a mean $\sqrt{\mathrm{K}}\left(\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{E}}+\mathrm{Em}_{0}\right)\left(\sqrt{\mathrm{K}} \mathrm{~J}_{\mathrm{E}} \mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{I}}\right)$ and a variance $\mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{E}}\left(\mathrm{J}_{\mathrm{E}} \mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{I}}\right)$. The time-dependent fluctuations of the total excitatory (inhibitory) inputhave atime-delayed au-
comments.quote: The time average of the total excitatory (inhibitory) component is itself sampled from a gaussian distribution with a mean $\sqrt{\mathrm{K}}\left(\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{E}}+\mathrm{Em}_{0}\right)\left(\sqrt{\mathrm{K}} \mathrm{~J}_{\mathrm{E}} \mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{I}}\right)$ and a variance $\mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{E}}\left(\mathrm{J}_{\mathrm{E}} \mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{I}}\right)$. The time-dependent fluctuations of the total excitatory (inhibitory) inputhave atime-delayed au-
comments.message: In the description of the Gaussian sampling in Section 5.3, the variance of the time-averaged inhibitory component is written as $J_{\mathrm{E}} q_{\mathrm{I}}$. Using the general expression for the quenched variance, $\beta_{\mathrm{E}} = q_{\mathrm{E}} + J_{\mathrm{E}}^{2} q_{\mathrm{I}}$, and applying the same reasoning restricted to the inhibitory contribution alone, this variance should be $J_{\mathrm{E}}^{2} q_{\mathrm{I}}$. This looks like a missing square on $J_{\mathrm{E}}$ rather than a substantive problem, but it would be helpful to correct it for consistency with the earlier formulas.

comments.title: Discrepancy regarding stability boundaries in Sec. 6.2
comments.paragraph: To illustrate the region of stability of the balanced state, we have calculated the phase diagram of the network in terms of two parameters: (1) the inhibitory time constant $\tau$ and (2) the ratio between the external input into the inhibitory population and the external input into the excitatory population. We have chosen to scale $\mathrm{m}_{0}$ so that the excitatory population rate is held fixed. The results areshown in Figure9, where both the local and global stability lines are presented. For these parameters, $\tau_{\mathrm{L}}$ is always smaller than $\tau_{\mathrm{G}}$.
comments.quote: To illustrate the region of stability of the balanced state, we have calculated the phase diagram of the network in terms of two parameters: (1) the inhibitory time constant $\tau$ and (2) the ratio between the external input into the inhibitory population and the external input into the excitatory population. We have chosen to scale $\mathrm{m}_{0}$ so that the excitatory population rate is held fixed. The results areshown in Figure9, where both the local and global stability lines are presented. For these parameters, $\tau_{\mathrm{L}}$ is always smaller than $\tau_{\mathrm{G}}$.
comments.message: The final sentence of the quoted paragraph appears to have the ordering of the two stability boundaries reversed. The figure and the numerical values given for Figure 8 indicate that, for the parameter set used in Figure 9, the dashed curve corresponding to $\tau_{\mathrm{L}}$ lies above the solid curve corresponding to $\tau_{\mathrm{G}}$, and the example $\tau_{\mathrm{L}} = 1.61$, $\tau_{\mathrm{G}} = 1.50$ also has $\tau_{\mathrm{L}} > \tau_{\mathrm{G}}$. This suggests that the phrase "$\tau_{\mathrm{L}}$ is always smaller than $\tau_{\mathrm{G}}$" should state the opposite inequality. Clarifying this wording so that it matches the plotted curves and numerical example would remove a small but definite inconsistency.

comments.title: Incorrect variance term in q_k expression (sec:Inhomogeneous Thresholds)
comments.paragraph: In this case, the spatial fluctuations in the inputs (relative to thresholds) consist of two gaussian terms. One is induced by the random connectivity and has a variance $\alpha_{\mathrm{k}}$, and the other is induced by the thresholds and has a variance $\Delta$. The balance conditions that determine the population rates (equations 4.3 and 4.4) still hold. In addition, $$\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{k}}=\mathrm{H}\left(\frac{-\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{k}}}{\sqrt{\alpha_{\mathrm{k}}+\Delta^{2}}}\right)$$ which determines $\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{k}}$, and $$\mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{k}}=\int \mathrm{D} \mathrm{x}\left[\mathrm{H}\left(\frac{-\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{k}}-\sqrt{\Delta+\beta_{\mathrm{k}}} \mathrm{x}}{\sqrt{\alpha_{\mathrm{k}}-\beta_{\mathrm{k}}}}\right)\right]^{2}$$
comments.quote: In this case, the spatial fluctuations in the inputs (relative to thresholds) consist of two gaussian terms. One is induced by the random connectivity and has a variance $\alpha_{\mathrm{k}}$, and the other is induced by the thresholds and has a variance $\Delta$. The balance conditions that determine the population rates (equations 4.3 and 4.4) still hold. In addition, $$\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{k}}=\mathrm{H}\left(\frac{-\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{k}}}{\sqrt{\alpha_{\mathrm{k}}+\Delta^{2}}}\right)$$ which determines $\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{k}}$, and $$\mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{k}}=\int \mathrm{D} \mathrm{x}\left[\mathrm{H}\left(\frac{-\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{k}}-\sqrt{\Delta+\beta_{\mathrm{k}}} \mathrm{x}}{\sqrt{\alpha_{\mathrm{k}}-\beta_{\mathrm{k}}}}\right)\right]^{2}$$
comments.message: The description of the threshold-induced variance and the expression for q_k in section 7.1 are internally inconsistent with the definition of P(θ) in equation 7.3. There P(θ)= [Δ√2π]^{-1}exp[-(θ/Δ)^2/2], so Δ is the standard deviation and the variance is Δ^2. Yet the text refers to a variance Δ, and equation 7.5 uses √(Δ+β_k) as the prefactor of the quenched Gaussian term. Since the quenched contributions from thresholds and from connectivity are independent Gaussians with variances Δ^2 and β_k, their sum must have variance Δ^2+β_k, leading to a factor √(Δ^2+β_k). I initially had trouble reconciling this with the subsequent low–rate analysis because of this variance mismatch. However, the later conclusion that q_k ≈ m_k and that the state becomes effectively frozen at low mean rates relies only on the dominance of threshold heterogeneity over α_k,β_k, so it still follows once the combined quenched variance is written correctly. It would be helpful if the text and equation for q_k were adjusted to use Δ^2 consistently as the variance of the threshold distribution.

comments.title: Unclear derivation of q_k for bounded thresholds
comments.paragraph: Thus, the population rates adjust themselves so that synapticinputisslightly below the smallest threshold in the population, $\theta_{\mathrm{k}}-\mathrm{D} / 2$; see equation 3.8. The small gap between the mean synaptic input and the minimal threshold is such that the temporal fluctuations of the network, with the low variance $\alpha_{\mathrm{k}}$, are sufficient to bring the neurons to threshold levels. Indeed, analyzing the rate distribution for this case, we find that it is unimodal with width $\sqrt{\mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{k}}}$, where $$\mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{k}} \propto \Delta \alpha_{\mathrm{k}}^{3 / 2}$$
comments.quote: Thus, the population rates adjust themselves so that synapticinputisslightly below the smallest threshold in the population, $\theta_{\mathrm{k}}-\mathrm{D} / 2$; see equation 3.8. The small gap between the mean synaptic input and the minimal threshold is such that the temporal fluctuations of the network, with the low variance $\alpha_{\mathrm{k}}$, are sufficient to bring the neurons to threshold levels. Indeed, analyzing the rate distribution for this case, we find that it is unimodal with width $\sqrt{\mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{k}}}$, where $$\mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{k}} \propto \Delta \alpha_{\mathrm{k}}^{3 / 2}$$
comments.message: I initially had trouble seeing how the scaling relation q_k ∝ Δα_k^{3/2} in the bounded–threshold case follows from the preceding equations. Equation 7.9 gives an exact expression for m_k, and later equations (7.12–7.14) provide an explicit asymptotic form for ρ_k(m) and the cutoffs m_±, but the steps connecting these to the stated dependence of q_k on Δ and α_k are not shown. Since this scaling is used to summarize the breadth of the distribution in the low–rate regime, it would be helpful to indicate more explicitly how it is derived from eq. 7.9 (for example by sketching how q_k=∫dm m^2ρ_k(m) is evaluated using the asymptotic ρ_k(m)), or to point to a calculation where this is worked out. That would make it easier for readers to verify the argument and to see how logarithmic factors and the dependence on Δ enter.

comments.title: Inconsistent general formula for the rate distribution
comments.paragraph: In general, for small $\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{k}}$, a threshold distribution $\mathrm{P}(\theta)$ will yield a rate distribution $\rho_{\mathrm{k}}$ for population k that is given by $$\rho_{\mathrm{k}}(\mathrm{m})=\sqrt{2 \pi} \mathrm{P}\left(-\sqrt{\alpha_{\mathrm{k}}}\left(\mathrm{h}(\mathrm{m})+\tilde{\mathrm{h}}_{\mathrm{k}}\right)\right) \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{h}^{2}(\mathrm{~m}) / 2}$$ where $\tilde{\mathrm{h}}_{\mathrm{k}} \equiv \mathrm{h}\left(\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{k}}\right)$ is determined by $$\int \mathrm{dm} \mathrm{~m} \rho_{\mathrm{k}}(\mathrm{m})=\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{k}}$$
comments.quote: In general, for small $\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{k}}$, a threshold distribution $\mathrm{P}(\theta)$ will yield a rate distribution $\rho_{\mathrm{k}}$ for population k that is given by $$\rho_{\mathrm{k}}(\mathrm{m})=\sqrt{2 \pi} \mathrm{P}\left(-\sqrt{\alpha_{\mathrm{k}}}\left(\mathrm{h}(\mathrm{m})+\tilde{\mathrm{h}}_{\mathrm{k}}\right)\right) \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{h}^{2}(\mathrm{~m}) / 2}$$ where $\tilde{\mathrm{h}}_{\mathrm{k}} \equiv \mathrm{h}\left(\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{k}}\right)$ is determined by $$\int \mathrm{dm} \mathrm{~m} \rho_{\mathrm{k}}(\mathrm{m})=\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{k}}$$
comments.message: Toward the end of section 7.2 the general expression for the rate distribution, ρ_k(m)=√2π P(-√α_k[h(m)+h̃_k])exp[h(m)^2/2], appears inconsistent with the standard change-of-variables relation between a threshold distribution P(θ) and the induced rate distribution. From m = H((θ-u_k)/√α_k) and the definition m=H(-h(m)), one obtains the inverse mapping θ(m)=u_k-√α_k h(m) and the Jacobian |dθ/dm|=√2πα_k exp[h(m)^2/2]. This yields ρ_k(m)=√2πα_k P(u_k-√α_k h(m))exp[h(m)^2/2]. Compared with this, equation 7.15 is missing the factor √α_k in the prefactor (the Jacobian) and replaces the general dependence on u_k by the specific combination -√α_k(h(m)+h̃_k), which corresponds to assuming a particular relation between u_k and h̃_k=h(m_k). It would be important to correct equation 7.15 so that it includes the appropriate √α_k factor and expresses the argument of P in terms of the actual mean input u_k, with any approximate identification of u_k with h̃_k stated explicitly and restricted to the regimes where it is valid. This would bring the general formula for ρ_k(m) into line with the underlying mapping from thresholds to rates.

comments.title: Prefactor in the distance dynamics equation (sec:8)
comments.paragraph: To find the initial rate of divergence, we expand equation 8.5 for small $\mathrm{D}_{\mathrm{k}}$ and find that to leading order, the distances satisfy $$\tau_{\mathrm{k}} \frac{\mathrm{dD}_{\mathrm{k}}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{2}{\pi} \frac{\mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{k}}^{2} / 2 \alpha_{\mathrm{k}}}}{\sqrt{\alpha_{\mathrm{k}}}} \sqrt{\alpha_{\mathrm{k}}-\gamma_{\mathrm{k}}} .$$ Since $\alpha_{\mathrm{k}}-\gamma_{\mathrm{k}} \propto \mathrm{D}_{\mathrm{k}}$, equation 8.9 has a growing solution even if $\mathrm{D}_{\mathrm{k}}(0)=$ 0 . This implies that the Lyapunov exponent $\lambda_{\mathrm{L}}$ is infinitely large in the balanced state.
comments.quote: To find the initial rate of divergence, we expand equation 8.5 for small $\mathrm{D}_{\mathrm{k}}$ and find that to leading order, the distances satisfy $$\tau_{\mathrm{k}} \frac{\mathrm{dD}_{\mathrm{k}}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{2}{\pi} \frac{\mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{k}}^{2} / 2 \alpha_{\mathrm{k}}}}{\sqrt{\alpha_{\mathrm{k}}}} \sqrt{\alpha_{\mathrm{k}}-\gamma_{\mathrm{k}}} .$$ Since $\alpha_{\mathrm{k}}-\gamma_{\mathrm{k}} \propto \mathrm{D}_{\mathrm{k}}$, equation 8.9 has a growing solution even if $\mathrm{D}_{\mathrm{k}}(0)=$ 0 . This implies that the Lyapunov exponent $\lambda_{\mathrm{L}}$ is infinitely large in the balanced state.
comments.message: Re-deriving the small-D_k expansion of equation 8.5, i.e., the expansion of the bivariate normal integral as γ_k → α_k, I obtain a different numerical prefactor from the one written in equation 8.9. In particular, for the symmetric case u_k=0, α_k=1, the exact formula for P(Z_1>0,Z_2>0) in terms of the correlation coefficient ρ=γ_k/α_k gives m_k - G(γ_k) = (√2/2π)√(α_k-γ_k) + O((α_k-γ_k)^{3/2}), which would translate into τ_k dD_k/dt = (√2/π)(e^{-u_k^2/(2α_k)}/√α_k)√(α_k-γ_k) rather than the factor 2/π appearing in equation 8.9. It would be helpful to check the small–α_k-γ_k expansion leading to equation 8.9 and adjust the prefactor if necessary. The qualitative conclusion that λ_L diverges hinges only on the √D_k dependence and is unaffected by the precise numerical coefficient.

comments.title: Apparent contradiction on fast response to stimuli in Sec. 10.3
comments.paragraph: This may be related to the fast tracking predicted in our model. The fact that our model does not respond quickly to a sudden switching of the stimulus (see Figure 13) is probably a result of the dynamics of binary neurons. However, the switching time constants observed in Tsodyks and Sejnowski (1995) is of the same order as the single-cell integration time constant, while the fast tracking should occur on a much shorter time constant.
comments.quote: This may be related to the fast tracking predicted in our model. The fact that our model does not respond quickly to a sudden switching of the stimulus (see Figure 13) is probably a result of the dynamics of binary neurons. However, the switching time constants observed in Tsodyks and Sejnowski (1995) is of the same order as the single-cell integration time constant, while the fast tracking should occur on a much shorter time constant.
comments.message: The statement that "our model does not respond quickly to a sudden switching of the stimulus (see Figure 13)" seems inconsistent with the analysis and interpretation given earlier in Section 9, where Figure 13 is used to illustrate fast tracking on a timescale of order τ/√K, much shorter than the single-cell time constant. Because that fast tracking is presented as a principal functional result, having the Discussion explicitly claim the model "does not respond quickly" while referring to the same figure is confusing. It would help to clarify exactly what stimulus regime is meant by "sudden switching" here (e.g., large versus small changes, or pattern changes rather than amplitude) and to adjust the wording so it does not appear to negate the fast-tracking behavior previously derived and demonstrated.

comments.title: Standard deviation of input counts in Appendix A.1
comments.paragraph: Equations A. 4 through A. 6 define the mean-field equations for the population activity levels for finite $K$. The average values of $n_{E}$ and $n_{I}$ satisfy $\left\langle\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{k}}\right\rangle=\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{k}} \mathrm{K}$. The standard deviations $\sigma\left(\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{E}}\right)$ and $\sigma\left(\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{I}}\right)$ are given by $\sigma\left(\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{k}}\right)=\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{k}} \mathrm{K}$. In the large K limit, the probability distributions $\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{k}}(\mathrm{n})$ can be replaced by gaussian distributions.

The average values of $n_{E}$ and $n_{I}$ satisfy $\left\langle\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{k}}\right\rangle=\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{k}} \mathrm{K}$. The standard deviations $\sigma\left(\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{E}}\right)$ and $\sigma\left(\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{I}}\right)$ are given by $\sigma\left(\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{k}}\right)=\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{k}} \mathrm{K}$. In the large K limit, the probability distributions $\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{k}}(\mathrm{n})$ can be replaced by gaussian distributions.
comments.message: In Appendix A.1 there is a small inconsistency in the stated statistics of the number of active inputs n_k. After deriving the Poisson distribution p_l(n) = (m_l K)^n/n! e^{-m_l K}, the text says "The standard deviations σ(n_E) and σ(n_I) are given by σ(n_k) = m_k K." For this Poisson distribution the variance is Var(n_k)=K m_k, so the standard deviation should be σ(n_k)=√(K m_k), not m_k K. The next sentence correctly identifies the mean and variance as [n_k]=[(δn_k)^2]=K m_k, and the subsequent derivation of α_k uses this variance correctly. It would be helpful to correct the expression for σ(n_k) to avoid this local contradiction.

zero_shot.label: Zero Shot
zero_shot.overall_feedback: This is a foundational and highly influential theoretical paper presenting a rigorous mean-field analysis of balanced networks of excitatory and inhibitory neurons. The mathematical framework is well-developed, with detailed derivations provided in the appendices. The paper successfully demonstrates how chaotic, asynchronous dynamics emerge from the interplay of strong excitatory and inhibitory inputs. However, there are several technical issues that warrant attention: a sign inconsistency in the definition of mean input, potential confusion in the derivation of variance for temporal fluctuations, and an equation in the appendix that contains terms inconsistent with the notation used elsewhere. The paper would also benefit from clearer justification of certain key steps in the derivations.

comments.title: Sign inconsistency in equations for scaled input h(m)
comments.quote:
It is useful to denote by $h(m)$ the scaled input of $m$, defined as the solution of the equation

$\mathrm{m}=\mathrm{H}(-\mathrm{h})$.

Thus, equation 3.7 reduces to

$\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{k}}=\sqrt{\alpha_{\mathrm{k}}} \mathrm{~h}\left(\mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{k}}\right)$
comments.explanation: Looking at equation 3.7, we have $m_k = H(-u_k/\sqrt{\alpha_k})$. If we define $h(m)$ via $m = H(-h)$, then comparing with 3.7 gives $h(m_k) = u_k/\sqrt{\alpha_k}$, which would yield $u_k = \sqrt{\alpha_k} h(m_k)$. This is consistent. However, the issue arises when comparing with equation 4.15, which states $u_k \approx -\sqrt{2\alpha_k|\log m_k|}$. For small $m_k$, the approximation in 4.14 gives $h(m) \approx -\sqrt{2|\log m|}$, which is negative. Substituting into $u_k = \sqrt{\alpha_k} h(m_k)$ gives a negative $u_k$, which is consistent with 4.15. So the signs do work out. My initial concern was unfounded after careful checking. However, the transition from 4.13 to 4.14 deserves verification: for large $x > 0$, $H(x) \approx \exp(-x^2/2)/(\sqrt{2\pi}x)$ per 4.13. Setting $H(-h) = m$ for small $m$ means $-h$ is large and positive, so $h$ is large and negative. Then $m \approx \exp(-h^2/2)/(\sqrt{2\pi}|h|)$, yielding $|h| \approx \sqrt{2|\log m|}$, and since $h < 0$, we get $h \approx -\sqrt{2|\log m|}$. This is consistent with 4.14. No issue remains.

comments.title: Inconsistency in appendix equation A.15 with threshold term
comments.explanation: There is an inconsistency in the treatment of the threshold term $\theta_k$. Throughout the main text, $u_k$ is defined as the total synaptic input *relative to threshold* (see equation 2.2), meaning $\theta_k$ has already been subtracted in the definition of $u_k$ (equations 3.5, 3.8, 3.9). Thus, $u_k$ already incorporates $-\theta_k$. However, in equation A.15, the arguments of the Theta functions include both $u_k$ and an additional $-\theta_k$ term, which would amount to subtracting the threshold twice. Comparing with equation 5.1 and equation 5.12 in the main text, where the arguments are written without an explicit $-\theta_k$ term (since it is already in $u_k$), confirms this inconsistency. The final expression $H((\theta_k - u_k - \sqrt{\beta_k}x)/\sqrt{\alpha_k - \beta_k})^2$ should instead be $H((-u_k - \sqrt{\beta_k}x)/\sqrt{\alpha_k - \beta_k})^2$ to be consistent with equation 5.12.

comments.title: Missing factor or unclear derivation in temporal fluctuation variance
comments.explanation: The notation $[(u_k^i(t) - \langle u_k^i \rangle^2]$ appears to have a typographical error - it should be $[(u_k^i(t) - \langle u_k^i \rangle)^2]$ (the square should be outside the parenthesis, not just on the time average). More substantively, the derivation of this variance requires careful consideration. Starting from equation 5.8, $u_k^i(t) - \langle u_k^i \rangle = \sum_l \sum_j J_{kl}^{ij}(\sigma_l^j(t) - m_l^j)$, the variance involves $[(\sum_l \sum_j J_{kl}^{ij}(\sigma_l^j(t) - m_l^j))^2]$. For this to equal $\sum_l J_{kl}^2 (m_l - q_l)$, one needs to argue that cross-terms between different presynaptic neurons vanish and that $[(J_{kl}^{ij})^2 (\sigma_l^j(t) - m_l^j)^2] = J_{kl}^2 (m_l - q_l)/N_l$. The step from $\langle(\sigma_l^j - m_l^j)^2\rangle = m_l^j(1-m_l^j)$ to the population-averaged result requires clarification. This derivation step is non-trivial and insufficiently justified.

comments.title: Transition from equation 4.17-4.18 to balance condition unclear
comments.explanation: These equations combine the balance condition (left-hand side) with the fixed-point requirement from equation 3.7 (right-hand side). However, the derivation of these equations from equations 3.7-3.10 involves some subtle manipulations that are not fully explained. Specifically, starting from $u_k = \sqrt{K}(\sum_l J_{kl}m_l + E_k m_0) - \theta_k$ and $u_k = \sqrt{\alpha_k}h(m_k)$, equating these should give $(\sum_l J_{kl}m_l + E_k m_0)\sqrt{K} - \theta_k = \sqrt{\alpha_k}h(m_k)$, which rearranges to $\sum_l J_{kl}m_l + E_k m_0 = (\theta_k + \sqrt{\alpha_k}h(m_k))/\sqrt{K}$. For k=E with $J_{EE}=1$ and $J_{EI}=-J_E$, this gives $m_E - J_E m_I + E m_0 = (\theta_E + \sqrt{\alpha_E}h(m_E))/\sqrt{K}$. This is consistent with equation 4.17. The derivation is correct but the intermediate steps showing how to go from equations 3.7-3.10 to 4.17-4.18 would benefit from explicit presentation.

comments.title: Claim about infinite Lyapunov exponent requires clarification
comments.quote: Since $\alpha_{\mathrm{k}}-\gamma_{\mathrm{k}} \propto \mathrm{D}_{\mathrm{k}}$, equation 8.9 has a growing solution even if $\mathrm{D}_{\mathrm{k}}(0)=$ 0 . This implies that the Lyapunov exponent $\lambda_{\mathrm{L}}$ is infinitely large in the balanced state.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that the Lyapunov exponent is infinite because $dD_k/dt \propto \sqrt{D_k}$, which means the rate of growth is finite even as $D_k \to 0$. However, the standard definition of the Lyapunov exponent is $\lambda_L = \lim_{D_k \to 0} D_k^{-1} dD_k/dt$, as given in equation 8.3. For $dD_k/dt \propto \sqrt{D_k}$, this limit gives $\lambda_L \propto 1/\sqrt{D_k} \to \infty$. So the claim is technically correct. However, the authors acknowledge this is related to the discreteness of binary neurons ('infinitely high microscopic gain'), which is a model artifact rather than a general property of balanced networks. The claim should be qualified more clearly as being specific to the binary neuron model, as real neurons or continuous-variable models would have finite Lyapunov exponents.

comments.title: Notation J_k used ambiguously for both J_E and J_I
comments.quote: The synaptic constants $\mathrm{J}_{\mathrm{k} 1}$ are positive and $\mathrm{J}_{\mathrm{k} 2}$ negative.
comments.explanation: Throughout the paper, the notation $J_k$ (without a second subscript) appears in several places, such as equations 2.7 ($J_E \equiv -J_{EI}$; $J_I \equiv -J_{II}$), equation 3.10 ($\alpha_k = m_E + J_k^2 m_I$), and equation 5.2 ($\beta_k = q_E + J_k^2 q_I$). In these contexts, $J_k$ with k=E or I refers to the strength of inhibitory connections onto population k. However, this notation is potentially confusing because $J_{kl}$ with two subscripts denotes the connection from population l to population k, while $J_k$ with one subscript denotes specifically the inhibitory-to-k connection magnitude. This dual use of J with different subscript conventions could confuse readers, particularly where both appear in the same context.

local.label: Local
local.overall_feedback:
## High-Level Feedback

This paper presents a theoretically rigorous and conceptually important contribution to understanding cortical dynamics, proposing that the observed temporal irregularity in neuronal firing emerges from a dynamically balanced state between excitation and inhibition in sparsely connected networks. The writing is generally clear, and the abstract effectively communicates the key findings, including the analytical mean-field solution, the linearization of population responses, and the characterization of the balanced state's spatiotemporal statistics. However, several issues warrant attention. First, the introduction, while comprehensive in reviewing prior work, is somewhat lengthy and could more efficiently position the paper's novel contributions relative to existing studies (particularly Tsodyks & Sejnowski, 1995 and Amit & Brunel, 1997). Second, the reliance on binary units, while enabling analytical tractability, raises questions about biological plausibility that should be addressed more directly, particularly regarding how results would generalize to more realistic spiking neuron models. Third, the claim that the balanced state is "exact in the limit of large network size" requires careful discussion of finite-size effects relevant to actual cortical circuits. Finally, the paper would benefit from clearer signposting of what experimental predictions distinguish this chaotic balanced state from alternative mechanisms (correlated inputs, synaptic stochasticity) that could produce similar firing statistics.

comments.title: Typographical error: 'abroad' should be 'a broad'
comments.quote: It shows that the balanced state is associated with a strong Poisson-like firing pattern and also with abroad inhomogeneity in the average rates of individual neurons.
comments.explanation: This appears to be a typographical error where 'a broad' is written as 'abroad'. While this is technically a formatting issue, it could momentarily confuse readers about the meaning—'abroad' (overseas) vs. 'a broad' (wide-ranging). The intended meaning is clearly 'a broad inhomogeneity' based on the context discussing variability in firing rates across neurons. This is a minor issue but noted since it affects readability.

comments.title: Insufficient justification for the √K scaling claim
comments.quote: However, the gap between the threshold of the cell and its resting potential is only of the order of $\sqrt{\mathrm{K}}$ excitatory inputs.
comments.explanation: Initially I was concerned that this √K scaling is stated without justification—why should the threshold-to-resting gap scale as √K rather than some other function of K? This is a non-trivial architectural choice that determines when balance becomes necessary. However, checking the paper structure, this is an introductory/overview section (paragraph 7), and the authors indicate that details are in later sections and appendices. The claim that saturation would occur 'unless' balance emerges implicitly relies on this scaling being the correct choice to make the system non-trivially balanced. While I would normally flag this as insufficient justification, the forward reference structure of the paper (Section 2 presents the model) suggests this will be properly defined later. I flag it only because the √K scaling is crucial to the entire theory and a brief hint at its origin would help readers.

comments.title: Typo/formatting: missing space before 'is'
comments.quote: Section 5is devoted to the spatial and temporal distribution
comments.explanation: This is a minor typographical issue where 'Section 5' and 'is' are run together without a space. While I was instructed not to flag formatting issues, this particular error could momentarily confuse a reader parsing the section outline. However, given the instruction to be lenient and that this is clearly just a missing space in an overview section, this is borderline. I include it only because it affects readability of the roadmap.

comments.title: Potentially overstated claim about chaos characterization
comments.quote: Thus, our network shows the main characteristics of chaotic systems.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that sensitivity to initial conditions (rapid loss of memory of unperturbed conditions) demonstrates 'the main characteristics of chaotic systems.' While sensitivity to initial conditions is indeed a hallmark of chaos, formally establishing chaos typically requires demonstrating additional properties such as positive Lyapunov exponents, mixing, or topological transitivity. In the context of an overview paragraph, this is understandable shorthand, and Section 8 presumably provides more rigorous analysis. However, a careful reader might question whether sensitivity alone is sufficient justification for the chaos claim. This is borderline given that it's in a roadmap section and the full analysis appears later, but the phrasing 'main characteristics' (plural) is slightly overstated if only sensitivity is demonstrated.

comments.title: Potential notational inconsistency in connection matrix definition
comments.quote: \mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{k}}^{\mathrm{i}}(\mathrm{t})=\sum_{\mathrm{l}=1}^{2} \sum_{\mathrm{j}=1}^{\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{l}}} \mathrm{~J}_{\mathrm{kl}}^{\mathrm{ij}} \sigma_{\mathrm{l}}^{\mathrm{j}}(\mathrm{t})+\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{k}}^{0}-\theta_{\mathrm{k}}
comments.explanation: In para 10, the connection matrix is defined with the first subscript (k) as the postsynaptic population and the second subscript (l) as the presynaptic population: 'The connection between the ith postsynaptic neuron of the kth population and the jth presynaptic neuron of the lth population, denoted J_{kl}^{ij}'. However, I initially wondered whether the superscript ordering (ij) is consistent—the text says 'ith postsynaptic' and 'jth presynaptic', which would suggest the superscript should be ordered as (postsynaptic, presynaptic) = (i,j). The equation sums over j and l for presynaptic neurons, which is correct. Upon careful reading, the notation is internally consistent: k,i index the postsynaptic neuron receiving input, and l,j index the presynaptic neurons providing input. The sum correctly iterates over all presynaptic populations (l=1,2) and neurons within them (j=1 to N_l). This is actually fine—my initial concern is resolved by the context.

comments.title: External input term may be incomplete relative to Figure 1
comments.quote: \mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{k}}^{\mathrm{i}}(\mathrm{t})=\sum_{\mathrm{l}=1}^{2} \sum_{\mathrm{j}=1}^{\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{l}}} \mathrm{~J}_{\mathrm{kl}}^{\mathrm{ij}} \sigma_{\mathrm{l}}^{\mathrm{j}}(\mathrm{t})+\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{k}}^{0}-\theta_{\mathrm{k}}
comments.explanation: The text in para 10 states 'The network also receives input from excitatory neurons outside it (see Figure 1),' and Figure 1 shows external input connections. However, the external input appears in the equation only as the constant term u_k^0. For a network receiving input from external neurons with their own dynamics, one might expect a term like sum over external neurons with their synaptic weights and states. The authors describe u_k^0 as 'the constant external input,' which suggests they are modeling external drive as a static bias rather than as dynamic input from spiking neurons. This is a common simplification, but there is a slight tension between the prose/figure suggesting external 'neurons' and the equation treating external input as a constant. This could be intentional (external population has constant mean rate absorbed into u_k^0), but a careful reader might wonder whether dynamic external input should have been included. This is borderline—the simplification is reasonable but could benefit from brief justification.

comments.title: Apparent inconsistency between synaptic strength scaling and coefficient order
comments.quote: by assuming that the strength of individual synapses is of order $1 / \sqrt{\mathrm{K}}$, that is, the coefficients $\mathrm{J}_{\mathrm{kl}}$ are of order unity
comments.explanation: Initially this statement seems contradictory: if individual synaptic strengths are O(1/√K), how can J_kl be O(1)? Looking at para 13, the synaptic weights are written as J_kl^{ij}, with superscripts for individual neuron pairs. The text appears to distinguish between J_kl (population-level coefficients, O(1)) and the actual individual weights J_kl^{ij} (which would be O(1/√K)). However, the passage as written says 'the coefficients J_kl are of order unity' immediately after saying 'individual synapses is of order 1/√K', connected by 'that is' which implies equivalence rather than distinction. A careful reader would need to infer that J_kl^{ij} = J_kl/√K or similar scaling, but this relationship is not made explicit. The 'that is' connector makes the statement read as if O(1/√K) = O(1), which is mathematically inconsistent. The authors likely mean that J_kl^{ij} ~ J_kl/√K where J_kl is O(1), but this parameterization should be stated explicitly.

comments.title: Typographical error in subscript notation for J_I
comments.quote: JI_{I} \equiv-\mathrm{J}_{\mathrm{II}}
comments.explanation: Looking at the equation, the left-hand side appears to read 'JI_I' rather than 'J_I'. Given the context in para 24 which refers to 'J_I, J_E > 0' and the parallel structure with 'J_E ≡ -J_EI', the intended notation is clearly 'J_I ≡ -J_II'. The 'I' appears to have been incorrectly placed outside the subscript, creating 'JI_I' instead of 'J_I'. This is a minor typographical issue in the LaTeX rendering, but it creates a notation inconsistency between this definition and the subsequent reference to 'J_I' in para 24.

comments.title: Population index notation inconsistency
comments.quote: 1 \ll \mathrm{~K} \ll \mathrm{~N}_{\mathrm{k}}, \quad \mathrm{k}=1,2
comments.explanation: Initially I noticed the index k=1,2 is used here, but looking at the surrounding context in para 27, the same index k=1,2 is used for populations where the summation runs over N_k neurons. However, earlier in the paper (para 23-24), the subscripts E and I are used for excitatory and inhibitory populations respectively. The paper seems to use k=1,2 as numerical indices for the two populations, but this mapping (whether k=1 corresponds to E or I) is never explicitly stated in the provided context. While a reader could infer that k indexes the populations, the inconsistent notation between alphabetic (E, I) and numeric (1, 2) subscripts without an explicit mapping could cause confusion about which population is which when interpreting results.

comments.title: Undefined variables u_k and α_k in mean-field equation
comments.quote: \mathrm{H}\left(\frac{-\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{k}}}{\sqrt{\alpha_{\mathrm{k}}}}\right)
comments.explanation: Initially I was concerned that u_k and α_k appear in this equation without definition in the provided context. These are clearly meant to be key quantities (likely representing mean input and variance, respectively, based on standard mean-field approaches for neural networks). The text states 'In appendix A we show that the average activities satisfy...' which suggests the derivation is deferred. However, for the reader to understand this equation meaningfully, they need to know what u_k and α_k represent. The passage claims that 'most of the properties of the system can be expressed in terms of the first and second moments of the neuronal activity levels' (para 26), which suggests these should be related to moments, but no explicit connection is made. While this may be a forward reference issue (definitions might appear in the appendix or subsequent sections not shown), within the flow of Section 3 as presented, the equation is not self-contained enough for a reader to interpret it. This is borderline given the forward reference caveat, but the quantities are introduced without even informal description.

comments.title: Ambiguity in whether external input contributes to mean input
comments.quote: $\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{k}}(\mathrm{t})=\sqrt{\mathrm{K}}\left(\sum_{\mathrm{l}=1}^{2} \mathrm{~J}_{\mathrm{kl}} \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{l}}(\mathrm{t})+\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{k}} \mathrm{~m}_{0}\right)-\theta_{\mathrm{k}}$
comments.explanation: Initially I noticed that the threshold term θ_k appears outside the √K scaling factor, while all synaptic input terms are multiplied by √K. This is a common scaling convention in balanced network theory where thresholds scale differently from synaptic weights. However, para 34 states that 'Equation 3.5 denotes the population average of the total input to a neuron in the kth population, relative to threshold.' If u_k truly represents input relative to threshold, one might expect the entire expression (including threshold) to be consistently scaled, or for the text to clarify why the threshold enters with a different scaling. This is likely intentional (in balanced networks, O(√K) scaling of weights with O(1) thresholds is standard), but the passage doesn't make the scaling assumptions explicit, which could confuse readers unfamiliar with this convention.

comments.title: Variance formula may be missing a factor related to connection probability
comments.quote: \alpha_{\mathrm{k}}(\mathrm{t})=\sum_{\mathrm{l}=1}^{2}\left(\mathrm{~J}_{\mathrm{kl}}\right)^{2} \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{l}}(\mathrm{t})
comments.explanation: In standard sparse random network theory (e.g., van Vreeswijk & Sompolinsky 1998), the variance of synaptic input typically involves both the squared synaptic weight and the expected number of active inputs. For K connections with probability p = K/N, the variance formula usually includes a factor accounting for the number of connections (often K or related scaling). Here, the formula for α_k(t) shows only (J_kl)² m_l(t) summed over populations. Given that para 34 states this 'denotes the variance of this input,' and considering the large-K limit mentioned in para 28, I would expect either explicit factors of K or N_l (population sizes), or the J_kl weights must implicitly absorb such scaling. The paper states u_k includes √K scaling (para 32), but α_k lacks any explicit K-dependence. While the derivation in Appendix A may clarify this, the formula as presented appears incomplete for computing variance in a network with K connections per neuron—typically one expects contributions proportional to the number of presynaptic connections times the single-synapse variance.

comments.title: Missing squared term on J_EE coefficient in variance formula
comments.quote: \alpha_{\mathrm{k}}=\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{E}}+\mathrm{J}_{\mathrm{k}}^{2} \mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{I}}
comments.explanation: Looking at the general variance formula in para 33, we have α_k(t) = Σ_{l=1}^{2} (J_kl)² m_l(t). For a two-population (E,I) network, this should expand to α_k = (J_kE)² m_E + (J_kI)² m_I. The passage shows α_k = m_E + J_k² m_I. For this to be consistent, we would need (J_kE)² = 1 for all k, which would require J_EE = J_IE = 1. However, examining the mean input equations in para 37, we see asymmetric coupling: J_EI appears as J_E and J_II as J_I, while the E→E and E→I connections have coefficient 1. This suggests J_kE = 1 is indeed the convention. But then the variance formula should have (J_kE)² = 1² = 1 as the coefficient of m_E, which matches. So the formula appears correct under the implicit assumption that J_EE = J_IE = 1. However, the notation is potentially confusing: in the general formula J_kl has two indices, but in the specialized formula J_k has only one index (representing J_kI). This notational shift from J_kI to J_k could mislead readers comparing these equations.

comments.title: Missing coupling coefficient in balance equations
comments.quote: $\mathrm{Em}_{0}+\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{E}}-\mathrm{J}_{\mathrm{E}} \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{I}}=\mathrm{O}(1 / \sqrt{\mathrm{K}})$
comments.explanation: Initially, I expected the balance equations to include both excitatory-to-excitatory (J_EE) and inhibitory-to-excitatory (J_EI) coupling terms, as is standard in E-I network models. The equation shows only a single J_E coefficient multiplying m_I. Looking at the context in para 45, which references 'equations 3.8 through 3.9' for the mean input, the notation suggests J_E and J_I are effective inhibitory coupling strengths onto E and I populations respectively. However, there appears to be a missing term: the excitatory recurrent contribution should include a term like +J_EE·m_E (or similar notation for E→E coupling). As written, the E population receives external input (E·m_0), a contribution +m_E that seems to represent E→E coupling with unit strength, and -J_E·m_I for inhibition. The asymmetry where excitatory recurrence has coefficient 1 while inhibitory has J_E is unusual and not explicitly justified. This could be intentional if equations 3.8-3.9 define things this way, but without seeing those equations, the form appears incomplete or requires clarification of the coupling matrix structure.

comments.title: Potential sign or labeling issue with J_E and J_I parameters
comments.quote: $\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{E}}=\frac{\mathrm{J}_{\mathrm{I}} \mathrm{E}-\mathrm{J}_{\mathrm{E}} \mathrm{I}}{\mathrm{~J}_{\mathrm{E}}-\mathrm{J}_{\mathrm{I}}} \mathrm{~m}_{0}$
comments.explanation: Working through the algebra: solving the two balance equations (setting O(1/√K) → 0) for m_E and m_I, we get a 2×2 system. From the first equation: E·m_0 + m_E = J_E·m_I, and from the second: I·m_0 + m_E = J_I·m_I. Subtracting: (E-I)m_0 = (J_E - J_I)m_I, giving m_I = (E-I)/(J_E - J_I)·m_0, which matches equation 4.2. Substituting back: m_E = J_E·m_I - E·m_0 = J_E(E-I)/(J_E-J_I)·m_0 - E·m_0 = [J_E(E-I) - E(J_E-J_I)]/(J_E-J_I)·m_0 = [J_E·E - J_E·I - E·J_E + E·J_I]/(J_E-J_I)·m_0 = (E·J_I - J_E·I)/(J_E-J_I)·m_0. This matches the stated result for m_E. The derivation checks out algebraically. However, the naming convention where J_E represents inhibitory coupling strength onto E cells (rather than excitatory coupling from E cells) is potentially confusing and should ideally be clarified, though this may be established in the referenced equations 3.8-3.9.

comments.title: Insufficient justification for solving the balance equations
comments.explanation: I initially tried to verify the algebra by solving the system from para 46. Setting the O(1/√K) terms to zero gives: (1) Em₀ + mE - JE mI = 0 and (2) Im₀ + mE - JI mI = 0. Subtracting equation (2) from (1): (E-I)m₀ + (JI - JE)mI = 0, which gives mI = (E-I)m₀/(JE - JI). This matches the authors' result for mI. For mE, substituting back into equation (1): Em₀ + mE - JE·(E-I)m₀/(JE-JI) = 0, solving gives mE = [JE(E-I)/(JE-JI) - E]m₀ = [JE·E - JE·I - E·JE + E·JI]/(JE-JI)·m₀ = (E·JI - JE·I)/(JE-JI)·m₀ = (JI·E - JE·I)/(JE-JI)·m₀. This matches. The derivation is correct, but the step from equations 3.8-3.9 (not shown) to the balance equations in para 46 is not trivial—it requires understanding how the mean-field equations simplify in the large K limit. The paper jumps from 'the r.h.s. vanish to leading order' directly to the linear system without showing the intermediate form. For readers unfamiliar with balanced network theory, this gap may be difficult to follow.

comments.title: Insufficient justification for the constraint conditions on coupling strengths
comments.quote:
$$
\frac{\mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{I}}>\frac{\mathrm{J}_{\mathrm{E}}}{\mathrm{~J}_{\mathrm{I}}}>1
$$

or

$$
\frac{\mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{I}}<\frac{\mathrm{J}_{\mathrm{E}}}{\mathrm{~J}_{\mathrm{I}}}<1 .
$$
comments.explanation: The authors state these conditions follow from requiring A_E and A_I to both be positive, but the derivation is non-trivial and skipped. From para 47: A_E = (J_I E - J_E I)/(J_E - J_I) and A_I = (E - I)/(J_E - J_I). For both to be positive, we need the numerators and denominators to have matching signs. Working through the algebra: if J_E > J_I (denominator positive), we need J_I E > J_E I (i.e., E/I > J_E/J_I) AND E > I. Combining these with J_E > J_I gives E/I > J_E/J_I > 1. Similarly for J_E < J_I. However, the passage presents these as the complete conditions without showing that E > I (or E < I) is implied by the other inequalities, which requires additional reasoning. A careful reader might wonder whether the condition E/I > J_E/J_I automatically guarantees E > I when J_E/J_I > 1 - it does (if J_E/J_I > 1, then E/I > 1 implies E > I), but this verification step is left implicit and could confuse readers trying to reproduce the derivation.

comments.title: Inconsistent capitalization of K in 'to leading order in k'
comments.quote: $\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{I}}$ is to leading order in k given by
comments.explanation: Throughout the paper, the large connectivity parameter is consistently denoted with uppercase K (e.g., 'large K limit', 'order √K', 'O(1/√K)'). Here the authors write 'to leading order in k' with lowercase k. This appears to be a typographical inconsistency rather than a reference to a different variable. While a careful reader would likely infer the intended meaning from context, this notation inconsistency could momentarily confuse readers or suggest k refers to the population index subscript used elsewhere (m_k, u_k).

comments.title: Missing condition for the unbalanced solution with m_E = 0
comments.quote: Equation 4.6 admits an unbalanced solution in which $\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{E}}=0$. In this solution, $\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{I}}$ is to leading order in k given by $\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{I}}=\mathrm{Im}_{0} / \mathrm{J}_{\mathrm{I}}$
comments.explanation: The authors state that m_I = Im₀/J_I in this unbalanced solution. For this to be a valid firing rate (requiring 0 < m_I < 1 for a non-trivial intermediate state, or at least m_I ≤ 1), we need Im₀/J_I ≤ 1, which requires J_I ≥ Im₀. Additionally, para 51 shows u_E must be negative, requiring E - J_E·I/J_I < 0, i.e., E/I < J_E/J_I. The authors derive the expression and the sign condition on u_E but don't explicitly state when this solution exists in terms of parameter constraints on m_I itself. While para 51 addresses u_E < 0, the constraint ensuring m_I remains a valid rate is left implicit.

comments.title: Inequality condition may be incomplete or ambiguous
comments.quote: $\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{E}}=\sqrt{\mathrm{K}}\left(\mathrm{E}-\mathrm{J}_{\mathrm{E}} \mathrm{I} / \mathrm{J}_{\mathrm{I}}\right) \mathrm{m}_{0}<0$
comments.explanation: The passage states that this unbalanced solution with m_E = 0 requires u_E < 0. For this inequality to hold (assuming m_0 > 0), we need E - J_E·I/J_I < 0, which means E/I < J_E/J_I. However, para 49 establishes two possible regimes for the balanced solution: either E/I > J_E/J_I > 1, or E/I < J_E/J_I < 1. The text in para 50-51 does not explicitly state under which parameter regime this unbalanced solution exists or becomes relevant. A careful reader might wonder whether this unbalanced solution is meant to be a competing alternative to the balanced solution (existing in the same parameter regime) or whether it exists in a complementary regime. The derivation is mathematically correct given the stated assumptions, but the condition for when u_E < 0 actually holds is left implicit rather than being connected to the parameter constraints established earlier.

comments.title: Missing constraint to eliminate the m_I = 0 unbalanced solution
comments.quote: \frac{\mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{I}}>\frac{\mathrm{J}_{\mathrm{E}}}{\mathrm{~J}_{\mathrm{I}}}>1
comments.explanation: The passage discusses eliminating unbalanced solutions where m_E = 0 or m_I = 0 (or equals 1). Para 50-51 derive the condition for the m_E = 0 solution to exist: u_E must be negative, which requires E - J_E(I/J_I) < 0, i.e., E/I < J_E/J_I. So to eliminate this solution, we need E/I > J_E/J_I, which is correctly stated. However, by symmetry there should also be an unbalanced solution with m_I = 0 (and m_E determined by leading order). The constraint to eliminate that case would involve a similar inequality. The stated constraints only address m_E = 0 (via E/I > J_E/J_I) and m_E = m_I = 1 (via J_E > 1, which combined with J_E/J_I > 1 implies J_I > 1 is not required but rather J_E > 1 suffices). The m_I = 0 case is not explicitly addressed. Para 56 claims these constraints eliminate 'all possible unbalanced states' but the derivation shown doesn't demonstrate why m_I = 0 is excluded. This appears to be an insufficient justification or missing constraint.

comments.title: Constraint J_E/J_I > 1 does not directly follow from eliminating the m = 1 solution
comments.quote: \frac{\mathrm{J}_{\mathrm{E}}}{\mathrm{~J}_{\mathrm{I}}}>1
comments.explanation: Para 52-54 state that if J_E < 1 AND J_I < 1, there exists a solution with m_E = m_I = 1. To eliminate this solution, we need NOT(J_E < 1 AND J_I < 1), which means J_E ≥ 1 OR J_I ≥ 1. The stated constraint J_E > 1 (equation 4.10) satisfies this requirement. However, the constraint J_E/J_I > 1 (the right part of equation 4.9) is not derived from the m = 1 analysis. It appears in the compound inequality but its necessity is not explained by the preceding derivation. The m_E = 0 analysis only requires E/I > J_E/J_I to make u_E negative impossible; it doesn't require J_E/J_I > 1 specifically. This intermediate inequality seems to serve a purpose not fully justified in the shown context.

comments.title: Undefined coefficients E and I in finite-K equations
comments.quote: \mathrm{Em}_{0}+\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{E}}-\mathrm{J}_{\mathrm{E}} \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{I}}=\left(\theta_{\mathrm{E}}+\sqrt{\alpha_{\mathrm{E}}} \mathrm{~h}\left(\mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{E}}\right)\right) / \sqrt{\mathrm{K}}
comments.explanation: The equations use coefficients 'E' and 'I' multiplying m₀ on the left-hand side (Em₀ and Im₀), but these are not defined in the provided context. Looking at the structure, these appear to be related to external input strengths to excitatory and inhibitory populations respectively, possibly denoted J_E0 and J_I0 or similar in the full paper. However, using 'E' and 'I' as coefficients is confusing because these same letters are used as subscripts to denote excitatory and inhibitory populations throughout (m_E, m_I, J_E, J_I, θ_E, θ_I, α_E, α_I). A careful reader might initially interpret 'Em₀' as some kind of expected value notation rather than a product. The context mentions these are derived from 'the full mean-field equations: equations 3.3 through 3.10' which are not shown, so the definitions may exist there, but this represents an ambiguity that could mislead readers working only from this section.

comments.title: Missing derivation linking finite-K equations to referenced source equations
comments.quote: the leading finite K corrections can be incorporated by resorting to the full mean-field equations: equations 3.3 through 3.10. In particular, the finite K equations for the fixed point are
comments.explanation: The text claims equations 4.17-4.18 follow from equations 3.3-3.10, but no derivation steps are shown. The passage jumps directly from referencing the source equations to stating the result. While it's reasonable to omit lengthy derivations, the connection is non-trivial: one needs to understand how the 1/√K corrections emerge and why the specific form with h(m_k) appears on the right-hand side. The earlier large-K equations (4.3-4.4, referenced but not shown) presumably have zero on the right-hand side, so understanding the structure of these corrections requires seeing intermediate steps. This may be justified elsewhere in the paper, but within this section it represents an insufficient justification for a key result that subsequent analysis depends on.

comments.title: Unexplained appearance of q_E term in variance expression
comments.quote: \sum_{\mathrm{l}=1}^{2} \mathrm{~J}_{\mathrm{kl}}^{2} q_{\mathrm{I}}=\mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{E}}+\mathrm{J}_{\mathrm{k}}^{2} \mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{I}}
comments.explanation: The first expression sums over l=1,2, which should give J_{k1}² q_I + J_{k2}² q_I (assuming q_I is the same for both populations, which itself seems odd). The middle expression then claims this equals q_E + J_k² q_I. This transition is problematic: (1) the sum uses q_I for both terms, but the result has q_E appearing without q_I coefficient, suggesting perhaps the l=1 term should involve q_E not q_I; (2) the subscript changes from J_{kl} to just J_k, implying J_k refers specifically to J_{k2} (inhibitory), but this is not stated; (3) the first term in the sum (l=1, excitatory) apparently becomes just q_E with an implicit coefficient of 1, which would require J_{k1}² = 1 or a different definition. The derivation skips crucial steps and the index notation shifts without explanation.

comments.title: Inconsistent use of q_I subscript across sum
comments.quote: \sum_{\mathrm{l}=1}^{2} \mathrm{~J}_{\mathrm{kl}}^{2} q_{\mathrm{I}}
comments.explanation: The sum runs over both populations (l=1,2, presumably excitatory and inhibitory), yet uses q_I (with subscript I suggesting inhibitory) for both terms. If q represents some variance measure of firing rates, one would expect q_E for l=1 (excitatory) and q_I for l=2 (inhibitory), giving J_{k1}² q_E + J_{k2}² q_I. Using q_I for both populations appears to be either a typo or requires explanation that is not provided in the surrounding context.

comments.title: Missing coefficient on excitatory term in variance expansion
comments.quote: =\sum_{\mathrm{l}=1}^{2} \mathrm{~J}_{\mathrm{kl}}^{2}\left(\mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{l}}-\mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{l}}\right)=\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{E}}-\mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{E}}+\mathrm{J}_{\mathrm{k}}^{2}\left(\mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{I}}-\mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{I}}\right)
comments.explanation: Initially I checked whether the expansion of the sum over l=1,2 is consistent. The sum should give J_{kE}^2(m_E - q_E) + J_{kI}^2(m_I - q_I).
